speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, after all the advice you received to forget, why is it that you persist in Holocaust remembrance and education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I was hoping that by telling the story, the world will learn, will know, and maybe will change. But my biggest disappointment in my life is that although the world knows now, nothing change, nothing changes. However, I have-- the biggest problem that I'm facing now as a survivor is when I hear reports from Bosnia, from Rwanda, from Somalia, Uganda, as we go through history, and I hear people say it is another Holocaust. My biggest pain when I hear three whales got caught in the ice in the Arctic, it is a whale Holocaust. They're poaching the elephants in Kenya-- it's an elephant Holocaust. There is no other Holocaust, there is only one. It is the first time in human history that a group of people, for no other reason than being born Jewish, were sentenced to be murdered. The first time in the history of mankind, the Holocaust stands by itself. There is no other atrocity. As tragic as the events of all these countries are, they can not, can never be compared to the Holocaust. 16 children were killed two weeks ago in Scotland, senselessly murdered by one crazed man. The whole world took note. The whole world stopped. All of England mourned the 16 children. It is a terrible tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think of my grandchildren. They are the same age, and I can't even think of something like this happening to me, to them. But I must at the same time think of the million and a half children that nobody blinked an eye. Did that-- they-- these children did not deserve to live? They did not deserve to be remembered? Did they just disappear? Did we ever think that a million and a half children could have made a contribution to this world? They could have been scientists. They could have entertained the world like Barbra Streisand.

speaker: interviewee
text: They could have been doctors. Maybe one of them would have found a cure for AIDS or cancer. A million and a half children died and the world didn't blink. And now there are people that say it never happened. The biggest pain is that the world had not learned its lesson. Yes, we do have a Somalia. We do have a Rwanda. And we do have a Bosnia and Croatia. This is the road the world took out of Auschwitz and continues on that road. Without Auschwitz, that would never have happened. It is a license to kill. And this is why we must educate the children, why they have to understand. And another thing that they have to know, that the Nazis were elected by the German people. Not all Germans were Nazis, but all Nazis were German. They were elected by the German people, because the people of goodwill did not bother to come to vote. And the warning that I give to our young people is this, don't stay home even if there is a blizzard. When you have the opportunity and the privilege to vote, you go and exercise that privilege. Because if you don't, you might wake up tomorrow, find yourself with a government that you didn't want, and it's too late to get rid of. In 1933, in 1935, in 1937, there was still time. Germans, the clergy, people of goodwill could have eliminated the Nazis. In 1938, it was too late. Even people of goodwill couldn't do it anymore. And this is my warning. People are full of hatred today. The racists are all out there. And if the Nazis would have finished the Jews and won the war, there would have been other victims that they would have chosen. Whether we'll have another Holocaust, I believe that as long as we have Israel, there is no possibility of having another Holocaust against the Jewish people. A little incident that I want to remind people about is when the hostages were taken in Iran. They were in no danger to be murdered by the Iranians, by Khomeini. They were very safe where they were held, for the whole world was watching what was happening. And if they would have been harmed, there would be no Iran, and we all know this. The next atom bomb would have [? fallen on ?] Tehran. The Jews did not have a country. There were no yellow ribbons on any old oak tree. There were no psychologists, psychiatrists taking care of the survivors. There were no hospitals where they were taken to, no receptions in the White House. The world did not want us, because we were the witnesses, and we were reminding them of what they didn't do and what they should have done.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you if you had a message for your children and your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope they continue my work. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's their-- we pass the torch, and they have to take the torch and carry it when the survivors are no longer with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, before we conclude the interview, I want to ask you if there's anything that you would like to add to what you have said today.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we have covered everything, more or less. The pain never ends. I think our bodies, as I said, were liberated on May 9. We will be liberated when we'll be laid to rest. We are still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, thank you very much for your most moving testimony today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well, on the right side standing is my mother's mother, my maternal grandmother. She-- next to her are her two sisters and her brother. And on the left side in a chair is her mother. The other lady is her mother-in-law. That photo was taken around the turn of the century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, if you could start on the right, your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, her name was Charlotte Brenner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else there you can identify by name?

speaker: interviewee
text: By name, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most probably in-- where they lived in Upper Silesia. Anywhere, in a park you can see this. OK. This one is my family. My parents, my sister on the right, myself on the left. That was taken in Krakow in 1939, probably June or July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, please identify everybody by name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. So you-- you have on the right crouching down is my sister, Hannah. My father, Joseph Steinitz my mother, Elfrieda Steinitz, and myself, Gerda Frieberg, then Steinitz. On the right side is my mother's sister, Alice Gerstel and her husband, [? Isma ?] Gerstel. On the left is my mother's oldest sister. Her name is Rosa Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their maiden names were Brenner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were the pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would not known when, and I would not know where. Probably they went to a photographer. On the left side it's my father. That picture was taken as a surprise for my mother's birthday in 1937. He ran to a photographer to take-- have this portrait taken. On the right is his sister, Friedel [? Kaufman ?], after marriage Friedel [? Weisenberg ?]. With her, baby, Klaus. And she's wearing a bracelet. That's the only thing that has survived her when she hid it. They took it away, and I have the bracelet now. This is the house where I was born and where my parents lived since they were married. The address was [NON-ENGLISH], which is 61 in Bielszowice. And you can see the display window and the door to the store. We-- we had all the-- most of the place. We lived-- there was only two people-- two other people that lived in that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country is Bielszowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was then Germany, later Poland. It's Upper Silesia. All right, on the right side, it's me, Gerda Frieberg, at age 8 and my sister at age 11. And we are on our way to go skating. That was taken approximately 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bielszowice, in my hometown. Well, this is my class picture in high school, what we used to call gymnasium, taken in 1937. And I am on-- sitting on the left side in the second row.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Explain it. ?] Where was your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was in [NON-ENGLISH]. And that must have been grade two-- grade one in-- in the high school, first-- first year high school. And these are my parents, Joseph Steinitz on the right and Elfrieda Steinitz on the left in Krakow, in 1939, early summer. Well, both pictures are of myself in the ghetto in [NON-ENGLISH]. The one on the left is on the street where I lived in the ghetto, and it was taken in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the closest city to [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was near Krakow. Well, this photo was taken a few days after liberation. In the background, you can see the barrack of our camp in [NON-ENGLISH], also the barbed wire fence. The picture was taken by the prisoners of war from South Africa which also now were free. And as you can see, we are wearing their hats. I am on the right, my mother in the middle, and my sister on the left. We got the clothes from the Russian soldiers who probably stole it somewhere, and we were able to get dressed and take our first picture in freedom. I guess we already in 1945 sensed that one day it will be important to have a document, for we asked the owner of the factory, in which we worked as slaves for three years, to give us some kind of a document that we can prove that we were working in his factory for three years. And so this is what it says in Czech, that we were working in that spinning mill during the time that we were incarcerated in a camp in Ober Altstadt. On the left is a watch from my grandmother, Ida Kaufman, and on the right is a bracelet from my aunt, her daughter, Frieda Kaufman Weissenberg. That bracelet was given to her on their first anniversary in 1931 by her husband. The reason that it survived, it is that they buried it in a garden of a German neighbor. And when we came back from concentration camp, these people called us over and asked us to take it out. And if they returned, we can give it to them, if not it, it would-- should be ours. There were a few other pieces, but this is all that I have. And it's priceless heirloom that I will leave for my children. It was customary for men to wear morning jackets, and this jacket is the only thing that I have left from my father. We hid some of our things with our neighbors, and after the war I picked it up. I have nothing else left. It was approximately 1920 when he had this made, and I remember him always wearing it-- wearing it on weekends and evenings. That drawing was sent to us from-- my-- my father sent it to us when we were in camp and he was in camp. It was made-- somebody drew his likeness in concentration camp. I'm assuming he wears the hat because they shaved their heads and he probably didn't want us to see him the way he looked. It was tragic for us to see that picture, because it is his likeness but is a far cry from how he looked when I last saw him. Yet, I am fortunate that I was able to receive this, how he was able to send it to us in camp, and I kept it all those years. Well, my mother was fortunate to survive concentration camp simply because we were together and were taking care of each other. This is-- this photo was taken at my daughter's wedding in 1973--

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when she was 72 years old in Toronto. This photo was taken in [INAUDIBLE], in Israel, at the Ben-Gurion University, when I established a chair in agriculture water management. I took my family down for this occasion. And we'll start with left, which is my sister, Hana Zimnowitz. Next to her is my daughter Josey Frieberg, her husband, Joel [INAUDIBLE], myself, Gerda Frieberg. Behind me, my son, Jack Frieberg, and my daughter-in-law, Sandra [INAUDIBLE]. That photo too was taken in [INAUDIBLE], in Israel, at Ben-Gurion University. And I'm on the left with my sister, Hana Zimnowitz. My son's children spent the summer of 1994 in Israel. And who we have here, the little one in front is the youngest. She's [? Orly ?], and she was 3 at the time. And to her left is [? Seth ?] who was 4 and 1/2 And then to the right is Matthew, who was 6 and 1/2. And behind him, the tallest is the oldest, is Adam who was 8. It was taken in Jerusalem in front of the [INAUDIBLE]. These are my daughter's two children. On the left is Jeffrey, who is about 3 years old here. And on the right is Julie Rose, Rose because of my Aunt Rose. And she must be 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their last--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken in their home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name-- that is Jeffrey Ostroff and Julie Ostroff. This is a poster that we used for the dedication of the Yad Vashem Holocaust Memorial. It's a sculpture that is in the [INAUDIBLE] park in Toronto. I was involved in the fund raising, general arranging, the [INAUDIBLE] ceremony, and then as chair of the dedication ceremonies. And this was presented to me in appreciation. I was chosen as woman of year by [INAUDIBLE] Women of Toronto in 1992, and-- and this is the award I was given in recognition of the work that I've done in this community since 1962. This is an award I received in 1988 from the Toronto Jewish Congress, the Jewish community, expressing their appreciation for my many years of volunteerism in the Jewish community. It's called the [NON-ENGLISH] award. This award I received in appreciation of chairing a small committee of survivors and being the founder of the Toronto Holocaust Education and Memorial Center. The candelabra I designed in 1971 when I was the [INAUDIBLE] chair and taught that it would be important that we have a logo that we can use with any Holocaust-related project that we do in the city of Toronto. That-- that has remained now the logo of the Holocaust Remembrance Committee and has been used since 1971. When all the program was finished in the Holocaust Center, I decided that there should be two sets of doors when we enter to put the visitor in the mood. So from the logo that I designed in 1971, I taken it to a sculptor and commissioned him to use that logo and create two sets of entrance doors to the Holocaust Center. It was dedicated in September of 1985 in memory of my father, Joseph Steinitz and my grandmother, Charlotte Brenner.
